item.title: Ep 1 · Ch 1 — Spiritualized Pain and God as Secure Base
item.description:
Chapter 1: A Brief History of Christians in Psychology
Explores the client who is torn between "I just need to pray more" and the guilt of seeking secular therapy, and shows how the historical split between Christian soul care and modern psychology plays out as an attachment injury in the room. Listeners learn interventions that validate the dilemma and reframe nervous-system understanding as honoring how the client was designed.
item.itunes:author: Psychology and Christianity Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 1: A Brief History of Christians in Psychology
Explores the client who is torn between "I just need to pray more" and the guilt of seeking secular therapy, and shows how the historical split between Christian soul care and modern psychology plays out as an attachment injury in the room. Listeners learn interventions that validate the dilemma and reframe nervous-system understanding as honoring how the client was designed.

item.title: Ep 2 · Ch 1 — Five Views, One Client: Assess, Align, Expand
item.description:
Chapter 1: A Brief History of Christians in Psychology
Turns the book's five positions (Biblical Counseling, Levels-of-Explanation, Integration, Christian Psychology, Transformational) into a working clinical sequence: identify the client's implicit camp, borrow their language to build trust, then gently widen the frame. Includes markers for cognitive bypass and how to translate theological vocabulary into attachment terms.
item.itunes:author: Psychology and Christianity Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 1: A Brief History of Christians in Psychology
Turns the book's five positions (Biblical Counseling, Levels-of-Explanation, Integration, Christian Psychology, Transformational) into a working clinical sequence: identify the client's implicit camp, borrow their language to build trust, then gently widen the frame. Includes markers for cognitive bypass and how to translate theological vocabulary into attachment terms.

item.title: Ep 3 · Ch 1 — When Scripture Becomes a Shield Against Vulnerability
item.description:
Chapter 1: A Brief History of Christians in Psychology
Troubleshoots the two failure modes of faith-informed work: colluding with spiritual bypass and authoritarian scripture use on one side, uncritical secularism that ignores spiritual resources on the other. Teaches the pivot from ideological content to protective process when a client says therapy is "just secular humanism."
item.itunes:author: Psychology and Christianity Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 1: A Brief History of Christians in Psychology
Troubleshoots the two failure modes of faith-informed work: colluding with spiritual bypass and authoritarian scripture use on one side, uncritical secularism that ignores spiritual resources on the other. Teaches the pivot from ideological content to protective process when a client says therapy is "just secular humanism."

item.title: Ep 4 · Ch 1 — The Therapist's Formation as Primary Clinical Instrument
item.description:
Chapter 1: A Brief History of Christians in Psychology
Draws on the transformational tradition to argue that the therapist's own character and spiritual formation, not technique mastery, is what sustains a secure base under pressure. Covers recognizing countertransference in a high-conflict couple session and using aretegenic self-regulation, transparent naming, and a post-session examen.
item.itunes:author: Psychology and Christianity Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 1: A Brief History of Christians in Psychology
Draws on the transformational tradition to argue that the therapist's own character and spiritual formation, not technique mastery, is what sustains a secure base under pressure. Covers recognizing countertransference in a high-conflict couple session and using aretegenic self-regulation, transparent naming, and a post-session examen.

item.title: Ep 5 · Ch 2 — Multilayered Reality: When Depression Feels Like Spiritual Failure
item.description:
Chapter 2: A Levels-of-Explanation View
Shows how the levels-of-explanation model lets a therapist validate a client's biological and psychological reality alongside their spiritual longing, instead of forcing a choice between medication and faith. Listeners learn both/and reflections, how to externalize the internal war, and how to ask directly about attachment to the divine.
item.itunes:author: Psychology and Christianity Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 2: A Levels-of-Explanation View
Shows how the levels-of-explanation model lets a therapist validate a client's biological and psychological reality alongside their spiritual longing, instead of forcing a choice between medication and faith. Listeners learn both/and reflections, how to externalize the internal war, and how to ask directly about attachment to the divine.

item.title: Ep 6 · Ch 2 — Self-Serving Bias as Attachment Armor in Couples
item.description:
Chapter 2: A Levels-of-Explanation View
Reframes confirmation bias and self-serving narratives in couples work as attachment-protection strategies rather than logical errors, so the therapist stops refereeing the facts. Listeners learn the pivot from "what happened" to how each partner organized the event in order to survive a perceived threat.
item.itunes:author: Psychology and Christianity Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 2: A Levels-of-Explanation View
Reframes confirmation bias and self-serving narratives in couples work as attachment-protection strategies rather than logical errors, so the therapist stops refereeing the facts. Listeners learn the pivot from "what happened" to how each partner organized the event in order to survive a perceived threat.

item.title: Ep 7 · Ch 2 — The Cycle, Not the Character: Undoing Attribution Error
item.description:
Chapter 2: A Levels-of-Explanation View
A case study translating Myers' persons-and-situations research into systemic couples therapy, showing how attachment distress is the ultimate powerful situation that overrides disposition. Listeners learn to externalize the cycle so partners stop pathologizing each other as narcissistic or cold.
item.itunes:author: Psychology and Christianity Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 2: A Levels-of-Explanation View
A case study translating Myers' persons-and-situations research into systemic couples therapy, showing how attachment distress is the ultimate powerful situation that overrides disposition. Listeners learn to externalize the cycle so partners stop pathologizing each other as narcissistic or cold.

item.title: Ep 8 · Ch 2 — Securing the Base When Families Fracture
item.description:
Chapter 2: A Levels-of-Explanation View
Reads a teenager's post-separation acting out as attachment protest and a symptom of systemic instability rather than a behavioral disorder. Covers assessing the family ecology of divorce and pivoting the work from fixing the child to reducing the parental conflict driving the symptom.
item.itunes:author: Psychology and Christianity Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 2: A Levels-of-Explanation View
Reads a teenager's post-separation acting out as attachment protest and a symptom of systemic instability rather than a behavioral disorder. Covers assessing the family ecology of divorce and pivoting the work from fixing the child to reducing the parental conflict driving the symptom.

item.title: Ep 9 · Ch 2 — Cloudy Spectacles: The Myth of Neutral Therapy
item.description:
Chapter 2: A Levels-of-Explanation View
Examines how a therapist's implicit religious, sexual, and family values leak into the room and get misread as client resistance. Listeners learn transparency checks, how to name their biases in supervision, and how to repair ruptures caused by value collisions.
item.itunes:author: Psychology and Christianity Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 2: A Levels-of-Explanation View
Examines how a therapist's implicit religious, sexual, and family values leak into the room and get misread as client resistance. Listeners learn transparency checks, how to name their biases in supervision, and how to repair ruptures caused by value collisions.

item.title: Ep 10 · Ch 3 — The Integration Stance: Neither Compartmentalizing Nor Bypassing
item.description:
Chapter 3: An Integration View
Stanton Jones's integration model as a clinical posture: why splitting faith and psychology into non-overlapping magisteria produces spiritual bypassing and emotional deadness in the room. Listeners learn scripts for naming the attachment fear beneath a client's "I just need to pray more."
item.itunes:author: Psychology and Christianity Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 3: An Integration View
Stanton Jones's integration model as a clinical posture: why splitting faith and psychology into non-overlapping magisteria produces spiritual bypassing and emotional deadness in the room. Listeners learn scripts for naming the attachment fear beneath a client's "I just need to pray more."

item.title: Ep 11 · Ch 3 — Borrowing the Technique, Refusing the Reductionism
item.description:
Chapter 3: An Integration View
A three-step method for critical/constructive integration: learn a secular model's mechanism, surface its philosophical assumptions about human nature, then keep the intervention and discard the reductionism. Includes red flags for deterministic language and a practice audit of your own theoretical orientation.
item.itunes:author: Psychology and Christianity Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 3: An Integration View
A three-step method for critical/constructive integration: learn a secular model's mechanism, surface its philosophical assumptions about human nature, then keep the intervention and discard the reductionism. Includes red flags for deterministic language and a practice audit of your own theoretical orientation.

item.title: Ep 12 · Ch 3 — When Genes and Grace Collide in Session
item.description:
Chapter 3: An Integration View
How to work with clients trapped between "my depression is entirely genetic" and "a real Christian wouldn't feel this way." The move is to stop debating the science or the theology and treat the false dichotomy itself as clinical material, resolving the panic underneath rather than the argument.
item.itunes:author: Psychology and Christianity Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 3: An Integration View
How to work with clients trapped between "my depression is entirely genetic" and "a real Christian wouldn't feel this way." The move is to stop debating the science or the theology and treat the false dichotomy itself as clinical material, resolving the panic underneath rather than the argument.

item.title: Ep 13 · Ch 3 — Staying Non-Anxious When Research Threatens Your Convictions
item.description:
Chapter 3: An Integration View
Self-of-the-therapist work for the moment a client's story or a new study destabilizes your theological framework. Covers the countertransference pattern of withdrawing versus over-accommodating, and why "all data is theory-laden" lets you engage findings critically without panic.
item.itunes:author: Psychology and Christianity Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 3: An Integration View
Self-of-the-therapist work for the moment a client's story or a new study destabilizes your theological framework. Covers the countertransference pattern of withdrawing versus over-accommodating, and why "all data is theory-laden" lets you engage findings critically without panic.

item.title: Ep 14 · Ch 3 — Untangling Causation From Agency in Sexual Identity
item.description:
Chapter 3: An Integration View
Jones's treatment of sexuality as the stress test for integration: how to separate what shaped a client's desires from what they may choose to do with them. Listeners learn to sidestep the twin-studies debate and work the grief, isolation, and fear of rejection instead.
item.itunes:author: Psychology and Christianity Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 3: An Integration View
Jones's treatment of sexuality as the stress test for integration: how to separate what shaped a client's desires from what they may choose to do with them. Listeners learn to sidestep the twin-studies debate and work the grief, isolation, and fear of rejection instead.

item.title: Ep 15 · Ch 4 — Makarios, Metaphysics, and the Ultimate Secure Base
item.description:
Chapter 4: A Christian Psychology View
Christian psychology treats human flourishing as a teleological question that no purely empirical method can settle, with the Sermon on the Mount functioning as a working psychology of well-being. Listeners learn to hear a client's theological guilt as a primary attachment fear and to use their spiritual language for emotional processing rather than bracketing it out.
item.itunes:author: Psychology and Christianity Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 4: A Christian Psychology View
Christian psychology treats human flourishing as a teleological question that no purely empirical method can settle, with the Sermon on the Mount functioning as a working psychology of well-being. Listeners learn to hear a client's theological guilt as a primary attachment fear and to use their spiritual language for emotional processing rather than bracketing it out.

item.title: Ep 16 · Ch 4 — Breaking the Retaliation Cycle With Poverty of Spirit
item.description:
Chapter 4: A Christian Psychology View
A synthesis playbook for de-escalating couples locked in an "eye for an eye" dance, mapping reactive anger onto EFT's secondary emotion and mercy onto access to primary vulnerability. Includes markers for spiritualized blame, the "plank" intervention, and setting up a "second mile" enactment where one partner risks unexpected vulnerability.
item.itunes:author: Psychology and Christianity Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 4: A Christian Psychology View
A synthesis playbook for de-escalating couples locked in an "eye for an eye" dance, mapping reactive anger onto EFT's secondary emotion and mercy onto access to primary vulnerability. Includes markers for spiritualized blame, the "plank" intervention, and setting up a "second mile" enactment where one partner risks unexpected vulnerability.

item.title: Ep 17 · Ch 4 — One Flesh Broken: Betrayal and Premature Forgiveness
item.description:
Chapter 4: A Christian Psychology View
A case study on infidelity where the offending partner weaponizes forgiveness ("God forgave me, why can't you?") to skip the grief the repair requires. Listeners learn to hold the mourning open, move shame into genuine remorse, and stage an apology that names the depth of the bond wound without demanding absolution.
item.itunes:author: Psychology and Christianity Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 4: A Christian Psychology View
A case study on infidelity where the offending partner weaponizes forgiveness ("God forgave me, why can't you?") to skip the grief the repair requires. Listeners learn to hold the mourning open, move shame into genuine remorse, and stage an apology that names the depth of the bond wound without demanding absolution.

item.title: Ep 18 · Ch 4 — When Therapists Pathologize Faith as Existential Avoidance
item.description:
Chapter 4: A Christian Psychology View
Unexamined secular assumptions—reading prayer as low self-efficacy or self-denial as pathology—produce alliance ruptures that clinicians misread as client resistance. Listeners learn to use empirical translation schemes that render goals like self-actualization in the client's own framework and to treat reliance on God as a legitimate secure base.
item.itunes:author: Psychology and Christianity Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 4: A Christian Psychology View
Unexamined secular assumptions—reading prayer as low self-efficacy or self-denial as pathology—produce alliance ruptures that clinicians misread as client resistance. Listeners learn to use empirical translation schemes that render goals like self-actualization in the client's own framework and to treat reliance on God as a legitimate secure base.

item.title: Ep 19 · Ch 4 — Agape as the Therapist's Anchor Against Countertransference
item.description:
Chapter 4: A Christian Psychology View
Explores how a therapist's own metaphysical commitments sustain presence when clients attack, despair, or trigger judgment and withdrawal. Listeners learn a re-grounding sequence—notice the internal tightening, return to the client's intrinsic dignity, re-enter the Tango—and why technique alone without a deeper well of compassion trends toward burnout.
item.itunes:author: Psychology and Christianity Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 4: A Christian Psychology View
Explores how a therapist's own metaphysical commitments sustain presence when clients attack, despair, or trigger judgment and withdrawal. Listeners learn a re-grounding sequence—notice the internal tightening, return to the client's intrinsic dignity, re-enter the Tango—and why technique alone without a deeper well of compassion trends toward burnout.

item.title: Ep 20 · Ch 5 — Doing Psychology in the Spirit: The Transformed Knower
item.description:
Chapter 5: A Transformational Psychology View
Coe and Hall's transformational model argues the therapist's own spiritual and emotional condition determines what they can perceive in the room, not just which theory they apply. Listeners learn why the "patchwork quilt" of stitching secular theory onto doctrine fails, and how unresolved fear, anger, or shame becomes a limiting horizon that distorts the client's reality.
item.itunes:author: Psychology and Christianity Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 5: A Transformational Psychology View
Coe and Hall's transformational model argues the therapist's own spiritual and emotional condition determines what they can perceive in the room, not just which theory they apply. Listeners learn why the "patchwork quilt" of stitching secular theory onto doctrine fails, and how unresolved fear, anger, or shame becomes a limiting horizon that distorts the client's reality.

item.title: Ep 21 · Ch 5 — Recollection, Confession, Contemplation: Repairing the Therapist's Instrument
item.description:
Chapter 5: A Transformational Psychology View
A practical playbook for Level 1 of the transformational model — presentation and recollection, honest confession, and contemplative discernment — as pre-session and in-session preparation. Listeners get concrete moves for catching defensiveness, resetting after being triggered, and grounding clinical presence in a secure base rather than expertise.
item.itunes:author: Psychology and Christianity Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 5: A Transformational Psychology View
A practical playbook for Level 1 of the transformational model — presentation and recollection, honest confession, and contemplative discernment — as pre-session and in-session preparation. Listeners get concrete moves for catching defensiveness, resetting after being triggered, and grounding clinical presence in a secure base rather than expertise.

item.title: Ep 22 · Ch 5 — The Old Testament Sage and the Gentle Answer
item.description:
Chapter 5: A Transformational Psychology View
The biblical sage models psychology as both descriptive and prescriptive — observing the "natural oughts" of the soul, like how a gentle answer turns away wrath. Applied to a high-conflict couple, listeners learn to trace the harsh start-up to withdrawal cycle out loud and to own the clinical authority to prescribe, not just describe.
item.itunes:author: Psychology and Christianity Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 5: A Transformational Psychology View
The biblical sage models psychology as both descriptive and prescriptive — observing the "natural oughts" of the soul, like how a gentle answer turns away wrath. Applied to a high-conflict couple, listeners learn to trace the harsh start-up to withdrawal cycle out loud and to own the clinical authority to prescribe, not just describe.

item.title: Ep 23 · Ch 5 — The Vice of Curiositas and the Blinded Therapist
item.description:
Chapter 5: A Transformational Psychology View
Epistemological pathology is what happens when the therapist's ego, need to be right, or knowing-without-love causes them to misread the client and blame the resulting flatness on "resistance." Listeners get a humility check, a scripted repair move, and language for bringing the blind spot into supervision.
item.itunes:author: Psychology and Christianity Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 5: A Transformational Psychology View
Epistemological pathology is what happens when the therapist's ego, need to be right, or knowing-without-love causes them to misread the client and blame the resulting flatness on "resistance." Listeners get a humility check, a scripted repair move, and language for bringing the blind spot into supervision.

item.title: Ep 24 · Ch 5 — Union With God as the Therapist's Secure Base
item.description:
Chapter 5: A Transformational Psychology View
Self-of-therapist work reframed: when the clinician draws stability from union with God rather than finite personal capacity, they can co-regulate trauma without disengaging, over-clinicalizing, or rushing to fix. Listeners learn dual awareness in session and how to turn countertransference into contemplative prayer rather than only analysis.
item.itunes:author: Psychology and Christianity Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 5: A Transformational Psychology View
Self-of-therapist work reframed: when the clinician draws stability from union with God rather than finite personal capacity, they can co-regulate trauma without disengaging, over-clinicalizing, or rushing to fix. Listeners learn dual awareness in session and how to turn countertransference into contemplative prayer rather than only analysis.

item.title: Ep 25 · Ch 6 — Master Desires: The False Loves Beneath the Presenting Problem
item.description:
Chapter 6: A Biblical Counseling View
Powlison's biblical counseling framework recast in attachment terms: the heart's "ABCDs" — fear of man, pride, love of pleasure — are false loves that stand in for an ultimate attachment figure. Listeners learn to move a session from situational stressors to the master desire driving the reaction.
item.itunes:author: Psychology and Christianity Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 6: A Biblical Counseling View
Powlison's biblical counseling framework recast in attachment terms: the heart's "ABCDs" — fear of man, pride, love of pleasure — are false loves that stand in for an ultimate attachment figure. Listeners learn to move a session from situational stressors to the master desire driving the reaction.

item.title: Ep 26 · Ch 6 — From Symptom Whack-a-Mole to the Double-Truth Intervention
item.description:
Chapter 6: A Biblical Counseling View
A synthesis play for pairing secular psychotherapy's empathy and listening with a biblical worldview: draw out the story, name the master desire, then validate the pain while offering an interpretive frame. Includes the pivot phrasing and markers (like the exhaustion marker) that cue the intervention.
item.itunes:author: Psychology and Christianity Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 6: A Biblical Counseling View
A synthesis play for pairing secular psychotherapy's empathy and listening with a biblical worldview: draw out the story, name the master desire, then validate the pain while offering an interpretive frame. Includes the pivot phrasing and markers (like the exhaustion marker) that cue the intervention.

item.title: Ep 27 · Ch 6 — Clyde the Physician: Fear of Man Behind the Drinking
item.description:
Chapter 6: A Biblical Counseling View
A full case walkthrough of Clyde, a 38-year-old doctor whose anxiety, drinking, porn use, and marital conflict trace back to appeasement and messianic pride rather than mere adjustment disorder. Listeners see the Psalm 40 enactment that shifted him from paralysis to honest, vulnerable disagreement with his mentor.
item.itunes:author: Psychology and Christianity Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 6: A Biblical Counseling View
A full case walkthrough of Clyde, a 38-year-old doctor whose anxiety, drinking, porn use, and marital conflict trace back to appeasement and messianic pride rather than mere adjustment disorder. Listeners see the Psalm 40 enactment that shifted him from paralysis to honest, vulnerable disagreement with his mentor.

item.title: Ep 28 · Ch 6 — Plundering the Egyptians: Descriptive Insight, Redemptive Framework
item.description:
Chapter 6: A Biblical Counseling View
How to use secular psychology's descriptive acuity without swallowing its philosophy, and how to avoid the twin failures of over-spiritualizing (quoting Scripture before you have listened) and psychologizing away moral agency. Includes a descriptive-versus-prescriptive audit of your own case notes.
item.itunes:author: Psychology and Christianity Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 6: A Biblical Counseling View
How to use secular psychology's descriptive acuity without swallowing its philosophy, and how to avoid the twin failures of over-spiritualizing (quoting Scripture before you have listened) and psychologizing away moral agency. Includes a descriptive-versus-prescriptive audit of your own case notes.

item.title: Ep 29 · Ch 6 — When the Therapist's Own Pride Runs the Session
item.description:
Chapter 6: A Biblical Counseling View
The therapist's ABCDs in the room: needing the client to like you, the messianic complex, and frustration when change is slow because it reflects on you. Listeners get a mid-session pulse check and repair language for when ego, not the client, is setting the agenda.
item.itunes:author: Psychology and Christianity Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 6: A Biblical Counseling View
The therapist's ABCDs in the room: needing the client to like you, the messianic complex, and frustration when change is slow because it reflects on you. Listeners get a mid-session pulse check and repair language for when ego, not the client, is setting the agenda.
